history.content: What is the Transformer architecture?
The Transformer is a sequence transduction model that relies entirely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions [1].

follow_up: How many heads does its multi-head attention use?
standalone_reference: How many heads does the original base Transformer use in its multi-head attention?

history.content: Tell me about the Transformer model.
The Transformer relies on self-attention to relate positions of a sequence, achieving state-of-the-art translation quality with far more parallelism than RNNs [1].

follow_up: How is its attention actually computed?
standalone_reference: How is scaled dot-product attention computed in the Transformer?

history.content: What did the Attention Is All You Need paper introduce?
It introduced the Transformer, the first sequence transduction model built entirely on attention [1].

follow_up: What does it eliminate compared to earlier models?
standalone_reference: What does the Transformer architecture eliminate compared to previous sequence transduction models?

history.content: What is BERT?
BERT is a bidirectional Transformer encoder pre-trained on unlabeled text and fine-tuned for downstream tasks [1].

follow_up: What are its two pre-training objectives?
standalone_reference: What are the two pre-training objectives used by BERT?

history.content: What is BERT?
BERT is a bidirectional Transformer encoder pre-trained with masked language modeling [1].
What tasks was it evaluated on?
It was evaluated on the GLUE benchmark, SQuAD question answering, and SWAG, setting new state-of-the-art results [1][2].

follow_up: How many parameters does the base version have?
standalone_reference: How many parameters does BERT-Base have?

history.content: How does BERT represent input sequences?
BERT tokenizes input with WordPiece and adds special tokens plus segment and position embeddings [1].

follow_up: What is the role of its special classification token?
standalone_reference: What is the role of the [CLS] token in BERT?

history.content: What is GPT-3?
GPT-3 is an autoregressive language model from OpenAI, evaluated extensively in zero-, one-, and few-shot settings [1].

follow_up: How many parameters does the largest version contain?
standalone_reference: How many parameters does the largest GPT-3 model contain?

history.content: Tell me about GPT-3.
GPT-3 scales language models to 175 billion parameters and shows strong task-agnostic performance [1].

follow_up: What learning paradigm does it emphasize that needs no gradient updates?
standalone_reference: What learning paradigm does GPT-3 emphasize that requires no gradient updates?

history.content: What is in-context learning?
In-context learning is when a language model performs a task purely by conditioning on instructions or examples in its prompt, without weight updates [1].
Which model made it famous?
GPT-3 demonstrated it at scale [1].

follow_up: In what three settings is it evaluated?
standalone_reference: In what three settings (zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot) is GPT-3 evaluated?

history.content: What is retrieval-augmented generation?
RAG combines a pre-trained seq2seq model with a retriever that fetches relevant documents at inference time [1].

follow_up: What are the two components that make it up?
standalone_reference: What are the two components that make up the RAG model?

history.content: Explain the RAG paper.
The RAG paper augments generation with a dense Wikipedia retriever, improving open-domain QA [1].

follow_up: What are the two variants introduced in the paper?
standalone_reference: What are the two RAG variants introduced in the paper?

history.content: How does RAG combine memory types?
It pairs parametric memory (the seq2seq model's weights) with non-parametric memory [1].

follow_up: What does it use as the non-parametric part?
standalone_reference: What does the RAG model use as its non-parametric memory?

history.content: What is LoRA?
LoRA freezes pre-trained weights and injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers [1].

follow_up: What does the acronym stand for?
standalone_reference: What does the acronym LoRA stand for?

history.content: What is LoRA?
LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) adapts large language models by training small rank-decomposition matrices instead of all weights [1].

follow_up: By what factor can it reduce trainable parameters on GPT-3 175B?
standalone_reference: By what factor can LoRA reduce the number of trainable parameters when adapting GPT-3 175B?

history.content: Why is fine-tuning huge models expensive?
Full fine-tuning retrains and stores every parameter per task, which is prohibitive at GPT-3 scale [1].
Is there a cheaper adaptation method?
Yes - LoRA is a parameter-efficient alternative [2].

follow_up: What's the core idea behind its parameter efficiency?
standalone_reference: What is the core idea behind LoRA's parameter efficiency?

history.content: How do you get LLMs to reason step by step?
Chain-of-thought prompting provides exemplars with intermediate reasoning steps so the model produces its own reasoning chain before answering [1].

follow_up: On what kinds of tasks does it show the largest gains?
standalone_reference: On what kinds of tasks does chain-of-thought prompting show the largest gains?

history.content: I heard prompting with reasoning examples helps math problems.
Yes - that technique substantially improves arithmetic and symbolic reasoning in large models [1].
What is GPT-3?
GPT-3 is a 175-billion-parameter autoregressive language model [1].

follow_up: How can LoRA help adapt it efficiently?
standalone_reference: How can LoRA efficiently adapt GPT-3?
